title: Topic 417: Why Historical Games Educate: Learning History Through Play
content:
### ABSTRACT

This paper embarks on a noble quest to legitimize the countless hours spent conquering digital empires and rewriting historical timelines from the comfort of a gaming chair. It posits that historical video games, often dismissed as mere interactive distractions, are, in fact, potent educational tools. We shall delve into the psychological underpinnings of learning through play, exploring how these digital sandboxes—rife with questionable accents and historically dubious fashion choices—can foster a genuine interest in the past. Through a rigorous, albeit tongue-in-cheek, analysis of popular titles and academic research, this paper argues that the road to historical enlightenment is paved with pixels, polygons, and the occasional rage-quit. We will demonstrate that while you might not be able to write a dissertation on the Peloponnesian War after a weekend of *Assassin's Creed Odyssey*, you'll at least know that the Spartans were really, really into kicking people.

### INTRODUCTION

Once upon a time, in a dimly lit room illuminated only by the glow of a CRT monitor, a young historian was born. Not in a dusty library, surrounded by leather-bound tomes, but in the digital fields of Sid Meier's *Civilization*. This budding scholar learned about the wonders of the ancient world not from a textbook, but by desperately trying to build the Pyramids before Gandhi nuked them. This, dear reader, is the absurd and wonderful reality of learning in the 21st century. The notion that video games can educate is no longer a fringe theory whispered among rebellious teenagers, but a topic of serious academic inquiry [4]. And why not? If a well-placed headshot in *Valiant Hearts: The Great War* can teach a teenager more about the horrors of trench warfare than a dry textbook chapter, then who are we to argue?

This paper will explore the seemingly paradoxical idea that we can learn profound historical lessons while simultaneously engaging in activities that involve, for the most part, clicking a mouse very, very fast. We will journey through the digital landscapes of ancient Greece, the American frontier, and war-torn Europe, all from the safety of our ergonomic gaming chairs. We will examine the psychological principles that make these games so compelling and, dare we say, educational. We will also confront the uncomfortable truths: the historical inaccuracies, the commercial motivations, and the ever-present danger of learning that the Vikings, in fact, did not have horned helmets (a truth that has shattered many a gamer's worldview).

So, grab your controller, suspend your disbelief, and prepare to have your assumptions about education challenged. We are about to embark on a scholarly adventure that is equal parts academic rigor and unabashed nerdery. And who knows, by the end of this, you might just be able to justify all those hours spent playing video games as "research."

### LITERATURE REVIEW

To suggest that one can learn history from a video game might seem, to the uninitiated, as absurd as learning quantum physics from a comic book. Yet, a growing body of research suggests that the playful nature of games is not a hindrance to learning, but rather a powerful catalyst. The venerable halls of academia are slowly but surely recognizing that the digital playgrounds of today are the classrooms of tomorrow. Or, at the very least, a really fun way to procrastinate on your actual homework.

At the heart of this discussion is the concept of "learning through play," a notion championed by psychologists like Kathy Hirsh-Pasek. In her work, she argues that we learn best when we are active, engaged, and find meaning in what we are doing [3]. Play, she contends, is not just for children; it is a fundamental aspect of human cognition. Hirsh-Pasek's "Six Cs" of learning—collaboration, communication, content, critical thinking, creative innovation, and confidence—are all skills that are honed in the crucible of a well-designed game [1]. When you're desperately trying to coordinate a flanking maneuver with a team of strangers in a World War II shooter, you're not just playing a game; you're engaging in a masterclass in collaboration and communication (albeit with more explosions).

The concept of "gamification," the application of game-design elements in non-game contexts, has also gained significant traction in educational circles. The idea is simple: if you can make learning as addictive as *Candy Crush*, you've won half the battle. However, as Hirsh-Pasek and others have pointed out, not all that glitters is gold. Many so-called "educational" apps are little more than digital candy, offering a sugar rush of entertainment with little nutritional value [3]. For a game to be truly educational, it must be more than just a series of bells and whistles; it must be active, engaging, meaningful, socially interactive, iterative, and joyful [3].

Of course, the road to academic acceptance has been a bumpy one. The debate over historical accuracy in video games is as old as the genre itself. As the folks at Past Meets Pixel have noted, the primary goal of most game developers is not to create a historically accurate simulation, but to create a commercially successful product [2]. This often leads to a blending of fact and fiction, where historical figures rub shoulders with fictional assassins and ancient civilizations are built and destroyed in the span of an afternoon. This has led some to dismiss historical games as little more than interactive fiction, a fun but ultimately unreliable source of historical knowledge. But as we shall see, even in their most fantastical moments, these games can still teach us something valuable about the past.

### ANALYSIS

The human brain, for all its magnificent complexity, is a sucker for a good story. We are wired to remember narratives, not lists of dates and names. This is where historical video games have a distinct advantage over traditional teaching methods. They don't just tell you about the past; they immerse you in it. They let you walk the streets of ancient Rome, sail the high seas with pirates, and storm the beaches of Normandy. This immersive quality is not just a gimmick; it's a powerful psychological tool.

At the core of this is the principle of active learning. When you're playing a historical game, you're not a passive recipient of information; you're an active participant. You're making decisions, solving problems, and experiencing the consequences of your actions. This active engagement triggers a host of cognitive processes that enhance learning and memory. You're not just reading about the challenges of governing a medieval kingdom; you're experiencing them firsthand (and probably dealing with a lot of angry peasants).

Furthermore, historical games tap into our innate desire for exploration and discovery. They present us with vast, open worlds to explore, filled with hidden secrets and untold stories. This sense of discovery is a powerful motivator, driving us to learn more about the world we're inhabiting. You might start playing *Assassin's Creed* for the parkour and the stabbing, but you'll inevitably find yourself engrossed in the history of the Italian Renaissance, poring over the in-game encyclopedia to learn more about the historical figures you've just met (and probably assassinated).

However, we must not be naive. The educational value of historical games is often a happy accident, a byproduct of good game design rather than a deliberate pedagogical choice. The commercial interests of game developers often take precedence over historical accuracy. As we've seen, this can lead to a distorted and oversimplified view of the past [2]. The danger is that players may come to accept these digital facsimiles as historical fact, leading to a generation of armchair historians who believe that the American Revolution was won by a man in a hooded sweatshirt.

This is where the concept of critical thinking comes in. While historical games may not always provide accurate information, they can provide a powerful platform for developing critical thinking skills. By encouraging players to question the narratives presented in games, to compare them with other historical sources, and to consider the motivations of the game developers, we can turn these digital playgrounds into laboratories for historical inquiry. The goal is not to accept what the game tells us at face value, but to use it as a starting point for a deeper and more nuanced understanding of the past.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Let's be honest, the real reason you're reading this paper is for the juicy examples. You want to know which games will give you the most historical bang for your buck. Well, you've come to the right place. Here are a few case studies that illustrate the educational potential (and pitfalls) of historical gaming.

***Assassin's Creed*** **Series:** This is the poster child for historical tourism. The series has taken players to the Crusades, the Italian Renaissance, the American Revolution, and ancient Egypt, to name just a few. The level of historical detail in these games is often staggering, with meticulously recreated cities and a wealth of information about the historical context. However, the series is also a prime example of the blending of fact and fiction. The central conflict between the Assassins and the Templars is entirely fictional, and the historical figures you encounter are often shoehorned into this narrative. So, while you'll learn a lot about the architecture of Florence, you might also come away with the mistaken belief that Leonardo da Vinci was a Q-like gadget-master for a secret society of assassins.

***Civilization*** **Series:** This is the granddaddy of historical strategy games. It's a game that lets you guide a civilization from the Stone Age to the Space Age, making all the decisions along the way. It's a game that can teach you about the importance of geography, the complexities of diplomacy, and the relentless march of technological progress. It's also a game where you can have Abraham Lincoln leading an army of tanks against a spear-wielding Genghis Khan. The game's simplified and deterministic view of history can be misleading, but it's also a powerful tool for understanding the broad sweep of human history.

***Valiant Hearts: The Great War***: This is a game that proves that you don't need photorealistic graphics to tell a powerful historical story. This puzzle-adventure game tells the story of four ordinary people caught up in the horrors of World War I. It's a game that is both heartbreaking and educational, teaching players about the realities of trench warfare, the use of chemical weapons, and the human cost of war. It's a game that will stick with you long after you've finished playing it.

***Red Dead Redemption II***: This is a game that is less about historical events and more about historical atmosphere. It's a game that immerses you in the dying days of the American frontier, a world of outlaws, gunslingers, and the encroaching forces of modernity. The game's attention to detail is obsessive, from the period-accurate clothing to the realistic wildlife. It's a game that can teach you more about the feeling of living in that time period than any textbook ever could.

These are just a few examples, of course. The world of historical gaming is vast and varied, with something for every taste and interest. The key is to approach these games not as infallible sources of historical truth, but as interactive texts that can be read, interpreted, and questioned.

### DISCUSSION

So, what does all this mean for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits? For one, it confirms what we've long suspected: we're all just a bunch of overgrown children who want to be entertained. The success of historical games is a testament to the power of narrative and the allure of escapism. We're not just buying a game; we're buying an experience, a chance to step into another world and become someone else for a while. This is a powerful motivator, and it's one that the entertainment industry has been exploiting for centuries.

The rise of "edutainment" is a natural extension of this. We've realized that if you can make learning fun, people will be more likely to do it. This has led to a boom in educational games, apps, and other interactive media. But as we've seen, there's a fine line between education and entertainment. The danger is that in our quest to make learning more palatable, we may be dumbing it down, sacrificing rigor for the sake of engagement. This is a tension that we will continue to grapple with as technology becomes more and more integrated into our lives.

Furthermore, the way we consume history is changing. We're no longer content to be passive recipients of information. We want to be active participants, to explore, to discover, and to make our own connections. Historical games cater to this desire, giving us a sense of agency and ownership over the past. This is a democratizing force, but it's also a chaotic one. The proliferation of user-generated content and online communities means that historical narratives are more fragmented and contested than ever before. We're all historians now, for better or for worse.

Finally, the success of historical games reminds us that we are, at our core, story-telling animals. We make sense of the world through stories, and we are drawn to narratives that are compelling, emotional, and meaningful. This is a fundamental aspect of our psychology, and it's one that has profound implications for everything from marketing to politics. If you want to capture someone's attention, tell them a good story. And if you want to teach them something, make it a story they can play.

### CONCLUSION

In the grand tapestry of human history, video games are but a single, pixelated thread. Yet, as we have seen, they are a thread that is woven with surprising strength and significance. They are more than just a frivolous pastime; they are a reflection of our innate desire to learn, to explore, and to connect with the past. They are a testament to the power of play, a reminder that the most profound lessons are often learned when we are having the most fun.

Of course, we must not get carried away. Historical games are not a substitute for rigorous academic study. They are a supplement, a gateway drug to a deeper and more nuanced understanding of the past. They are a spark that can ignite a lifelong passion for history, a passion that will lead us to the dusty libraries and the leather-bound tomes that we so desperately need.

So, the next time you see someone glued to their screen, conquering digital empires and rewriting historical timelines, don't be so quick to judge. They might just be the next great historian, learning their craft one pixel at a time. And if not, well, at least they're having fun. And in this day and age, that's something to be celebrated.
references:
[1] Hirsh-Pasek, K., & Golinkoff, R. M. (2003). *Einstein never used flash cards: How our children really learn--and why they need to play more and memorize less*. Rodale.

[2] Past Meets Pixel. (2024, April 14). *Exploring the Potential and Challenges of Historical Video Games*. [https://pastmeetspixel.substack.com/p/exploring-the-potential-and-challenges](https://pastmeetspixel.substack.com/p/exploring-the-potential-and-challenges)

[3] American Psychological Association. (n.d.). *Why we learn best through play*. [https://www.apa.org/news/podcasts/speaking-of-psychology/play](https://www.apa.org/news/podcasts/speaking-of-psychology/play)

[4] Farber, M. (2022, December 12). Boosting Student Interest in History Through Video Games. *Edutopia*. [https://www.edutopia.org/article/boosting-student-interest-history-video-games/](https://www.edutopia.org/article/boosting-student-interest-history-video-games/)
